channel.title: Posts on Christopher Hannan
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Christopher Hannan

item.title: Cisco ACI - Leaf Switch Replacement
item.description: Note: Before following any of the steps below, please take note of the POD ID, Node ID and Node Name of the switch to be replaced. When you unbox the new switch, also note down the serial number of the new switch. Leaf Switch Replacement Power on the new switch and connect a console cable to check if it is running in ACI mode or NX-OS mode. If it is running in NX-OS mode, follow the steps documented in Cisco ACI - Converting NX-OS to ACI Boot Mode to convert the switch to ACI mode.
item.content:
Note: Before following any of the steps below, please take note of the POD ID, Node ID and Node Name of the switch to be replaced. When you unbox the new switch, also note down the serial number of the new switch.

Leaf Switch Replacement

Power on the new switch and connect a console cable to check if it is running in ACI mode or NX-OS mode. If it is running in NX-OS mode, follow the steps documented in Cisco ACI - Converting NX-OS to ACI Boot Mode to convert the switch to ACI mode.

1. Decommission the faulty switch and remove it from the controller

  1. In the ACI GUI, navigate to Fabric > Inventory > Fabric Membership, identify the switch to be replaced.
  2. Right click on the switch to be replaced and select Decommission. The status will show as decommissioned.
  3. Right click on the decommissioned switch and select Remove from Controller. The status will show as decommissioning and this process can take up to 10 minutes.

2. Commission the new switch

Note: Make sure that the new leaf switch is connected to all of the spine switches in the fabric.

  1. In the ACI GUI, navigate to Fabric > Inventory > Fabric Membership > Nodes Pending Registration and look for a new switch that doesn’t have an IP address or node ID assigned. Confirm it is the new switch by verifying that the serial number matches what you noted earlier.
  2. Right click on the new switch to and select Register. Configure the POD ID, Node ID and Node Name. Click Register. The status will go from unknown to discovered and the APIC will assign it an IP address and add it to the Registered Nodes tab.
  3. On the Registered Nodes tab, right click on the new switch and select Commission. The status will go to active.

3. Verify the new switch status

  1. In the ACI GUI, navigate to Fabric > Inventory > Topology to verify that the new switch is now part of the topology.
  2. SSH to the APIC and run the command acidiag fnvread to confirm that the new switch shows up as “active”.

4. Updating your known_hosts file on the APIC

If you try to SSH to the new leaf switch from the APIC and you have connected to the faulty leaf switch previously via SSH, permission will be denied and you will be presented with warnings about “POSSIBLE DNS SPOOFING” and “REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED”.

This is because the RSA host key has changed for the replacement leaf switch and does not match the RSA host key in known_hosts. To fix this you need to remove the RSA host key from your known_hosts file on the APIC as follows:

apic1# bash
cph-local@apic1:~> ssh-keygen -R <leaf-switch-hostname>
# Host <leaf-switch-hostname> found: line 2
/home/cph-local/.ssh/known_hosts updated.
Original contents retained as /home/cph-local/.ssh/known_hosts.old
cph-local@apic1:~> exit
exit
apic1# ssh <leaf-switch-hostname>
Warning: Permanently added '<leaf-switch-hostname>,100.64.64.64' (RSA) to the list of known hosts.

item.title: Cisco ACI - Converting NX-OS to ACI Boot Mode
item.description: Converting NX-OS to ACI Boot Mode Follow the steps below to convert a Cisco Nexus 9000 Series switch from NX-OS standalone mode to ACI boot mode. 1. Verify which mode the switch is running (ACI or NX-OS) Power on the new switch and connect a console to check if it is running in ACI mode or NX-OS mode. Cisco NX-OS filenames begin with “n9000” while ACI filenames begin with “aci-n9000”:
item.content:
Converting NX-OS to ACI Boot Mode

Follow the steps below to convert a Cisco Nexus 9000 Series switch from NX-OS standalone mode to ACI boot mode.

1. Verify which mode the switch is running (ACI or NX-OS)

Power on the new switch and connect a console to check if it is running in ACI mode or NX-OS mode. Cisco NX-OS filenames begin with “n9000” while ACI filenames begin with “aci-n9000”:

switch# show version

2. Uploading the ACI image

The ACI image can be copied to the switch directly via USB or via SCP from the APIC or an SCP server.

Method 1: Using USB

Copy the image from the USB drive to bootflash:
switch# dir usb1:
switch# copy usb1:<aci-image.bin> bootflash:

Method 2: Using SCP from the APIC

Configure management connectivity to the APIC:

From the APIC CLI, view the ACI Out-of-band information and find a spare IP in the management network to use on the new switch:

apic1# show oob-mgmt

Set the IP address on the mgmt0 interface of the new switch to allow management connectivity between the new switch and the APIC:

Example:

switch(config)# interface mgmt0

switch(config-if)# ip address <ip-address/length>

Patch interface mgmt0 on the new switch into the management network. Confirm connectivity from the new switch to the APIC:

switch# ping <apic-ip-address>
Copy the ACI image from the APIC to the switch:

Enable SCP services on the switch:

switch(config)# feature scp-server

From the APIC CLI, view and take note of the currently installed leaf/spine switch firmware versions:

apic1# show versions

View the firmware images present in the APIC repository and find the “aci” version of the above switch firmware. Take note of the MD5 checksum:

apic1# show firmware repository detail

Use SCP to copy the firmware image from the APIC repository to the active supervisor module on the switch:

apic1# scp -r /firmware/fwrepos/fwrepo/<aci-image.bin> admin@<switch-ip-address>:<aci-image.bin>

Note: For dual-supervisor systems, copy the ACI image from the active to the standby supervisor module. Example: switch(config)# copy bootflash:<aci-image.bin> bootflash://sup-standby/

3. Boot the switch to the ACI image

Configure the switch to not boot from Cisco NX-OS:

switch(config)# no boot nxos

Save the configuration:

switch(config)# copy running-config startup-config

Note: You must run the copy running-config startup-config command prior to booting the ACI image. Do not run it after you enter the boot aci command.

Boot the switch with the ACI image:

switch(config)# boot aci bootflash:<aci-image.bin>

Caution: Do not enter the copy running-config startup-config command after the boot aci command. If you do, the switch will go to the loader> prompt.

Verify the integrity of the file by displaying the MD5 checksum and comparing it to that noted in Step 3:

switch(config)# show file bootflash:<aci-image.bin> md5sum

Check the output of the show boot command to see if the bootvar is correctly set:

switch# show boot

Reload the switch:

switch# reload

Note: This reload and conversion can take up to 30 minutes to complete.

Log in to the switch as an administrator to verify it is now running in ACI mode. Notice that the fabric discovery process has been started at this point. It may be some minutes before the services start and we are able to access the switch via the console.:

Example:

User Access Verification
(none) login: admin
*****************************************************************************
    Fabric discovery in progress, show commands are not fully functional
    Logout and Login after discovery to continue to use show commands.
*****************************************************************************

You can now log in to the APIC and follow the steps documented in Cisco ACI - Leaf Switch Replacement to register and commission the new switch in the ACI Fabric.

Note: When commissioned, the new switch may show a degraded health and list faults for a firmware version mismatch of both the BIOS and FPGA firmware. This is because a conversion to ACI basically just boots the switch into the ACI image and, since no install scripts are acting on the box, all the auxiliary steps (that would be performed during a normal upgrade) are not done.

Normally these faults do not pose an issue and the BIOS and FPGA will be upgraded with the next ACI upgrade administered from the APIC. Alternatively, the BIOS and FPGA firmware can be manually upgraded as follows:

FPGA Upgrade (requires a reload) /bin/check-fpga.sh FpGaDoWnGrAdE /usr/sbin/chassis-power-cycle.sh

BIOS Upgrade (requires Cisco TAC) For the manual upgrade of the BIOS you will need to raise a service request with Cisco so that one of their TAC engineers can assist via a Webex session. There’s an enhancement filed to improve this to make the upgrade automatic, however, it’s not implemented yet (CSCvm40571).

Workaround Another option to address this issue is to convert the new switch to an ACI firmware version that is N -1. The switch can then be added to the Fabric and auto-upgraded from the APIC to the correct ACI, BIOS and FPGA versions.

item.title: Cisco ACI - Basic Commands
item.description: General Overview acidiag fnvread Displays the address and state of switch nodes registered with the fabric. Moquery Usage moquery --help usage: Command line cousin to visore [-h] [-i HOST] [-p PORT] [-d DN] [-c KLASS] [-f FILTER] [-a ATTRS] [-o OUTPUT] [-u USER] [-x [OPTIONS [OPTIONS ...]]] optional arguments: -h, --help show this help message and exit -i HOST, --host HOST Hostname or ip of apic -p PORT, --port PORT REST server port -d DN, --dn DN dn of the mo -c KLASS, --klass KLASS comma seperated class names to query -f FILTER, --filter FILTER property filter to accept/reject mos -a ATTRS, --attrs ATTRS type of attributes to display (config, all) -o OUTPUT, --output OUTPUT Display format (block, table, xml, json) -u USER, --user USER User name -x [OPTIONS [OPTIONS .
item.content:
General Overview

acidiag fnvread Displays the address and state of switch nodes registered with the fabric.

Moquery Usage

moquery --help
usage: Command line cousin to visore [-h] [-i HOST] [-p PORT] [-d DN]
                                     [-c KLASS] [-f FILTER] [-a ATTRS]
                                     [-o OUTPUT] [-u USER]
                                     [-x [OPTIONS [OPTIONS ...]]]

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  -i HOST, --host HOST  Hostname or ip of apic
  -p PORT, --port PORT  REST server port
  -d DN, --dn DN        dn of the mo
  -c KLASS, --klass KLASS
                        comma seperated class names to query
  -f FILTER, --filter FILTER
                        property filter to accept/reject mos
  -a ATTRS, --attrs ATTRS
                        type of attributes to display (config, all)
  -o OUTPUT, --output OUTPUT
                        Display format (block, table, xml, json)
  -u USER, --user USER  User name
  -x [OPTIONS [OPTIONS ...]], --options [OPTIONS [OPTIONS ...]]
                        Extra options to the query

Get help

moquery -h
or
man moquery

Query all tenants

moquery -c fvTenant -o table

Query specific tenant

moquery -d uni/tn-TenantTest123

Using moquery to check/sort active faults (faultInst)
moquery -c faultInst | egrep -e "^descr" | sort | uniq -c
or
moquery -c fvTenant -f 'fv.Tenant.name=="TenantTest123"'
or
moquery -c fvTenant -x 'query-target-filter=eq(fvTenant.name,"TenantTest123")'

Query specific tenant and all children

moquery -d uni/tn-TenantTest123 -x "rsp-subtree=children rsp-subtree-class=fvBD"

Query active faults

moquery -c faultInst | egrep -e "^descr" | sort | uniq -c

Query faults by description

moquery –c faultInst –f fault.Inst.descr==“: Failed to form relation to MO AbsGraph-VEStandAloneFuncProfile …”

Syslog

Generate a custom syslog message:
logit severity <severity> dest-grp <WORD> server <Remote Dest Name> <Syslog message> node <Source node>

Example:
logit severity 1 dest-grp splunk server 10.180.163.54 "THIS IS A TEST"

Local Users

apic1# configure terminal 
apic1(config)# username user5
apic1(config-username)# first-name George
apic1(config-username)# last-name Washington
apic1(config-username)# email gwashington@exampleCorp.com
apic1(config-username)# phone 14085551212
apic1(config-username)# account-status active
apic1(config-username)# domain mySecDomain
apic1(config-username)# clear-pwd-history
apic1(config-username)# expires
apic1(config-username)# expiration 2017-12-31T23:59+08:00
apic1(config-username)# password 
Password: c1$c0123
apic1(config-username)# pwd-lifetime 90
apic1(config-username)# domain mySecDomain
apic1(config-domain)# role tenant-admin
apic1(config-role)# priv-type writePriv
apic1(config-role)# exit
apic1(config-domain)# exit
apic1(config-username)# show username user5
UserName                : user5
First-Name              : George
Last-Name               : Washington
Email                   : gwashington@exampleCorp.com
Acount Status           : active
Password strength check : yes
